# Time Turning

Date and Time values in the days of yore are a fuzzy concept. A campaign will cross entire mountain ranges in a single fortnight, while waiting for months between sessions for actual story progress.

The important realization is that time in one universe does not have to travel at the same pace as time in another.

When merging backstories from different worlds, it's important to have a consistent entity which can be used to identify versions of the same character.

If your character is a shape-shifter, this can be especially challenging as visual information from one day to the next cannot be trusted.

How then my friends will we build a coherent story from disjoint pieces of writing. How will we handle situations where timelines contradict and human memory falters? 

The answer, my friend, is to make use of the oldest invention in human history, the humble pen and paper. With this new technology we cross oceans and visit distant star systems. 

## The Nighbloom Family

Mrs. Judeth Nightbloom is a teacher at Sunstone Mage College. She is an elvish mage and her given name is Judeth Ravenmark. This school sits along a rocky outcrop along the eastern coast of Atlantia.

The city of Atlantia contains a quiet watch tower run by the Ravenmark family, but Judith found the job tiring. 
Distant relatives of hers still staff the watch tower to this day, although it's no longer considered to be just a man's job. 
Old thought patterns die hard, and Judeth always imagined sailing to be a job more befitting of a gentleman than a dainty lady. 

## The Ravenmark Family
Judeth's cousin Mary Ravenmark died at sea at a young age, and her husband Paul Ravenmark never got over the loss. 

It was sad to see him drink away his troubles at the local pub. Atlantia is a small town, and when Judeth passed him in the street, 
she always tried to be kind, but eventually Paul's alcoholism got the better of him and he passed away. His daughter Jory still believes he was taken away by a see monster, 
but in away alcohol is its own kind of monster, and blaming her fathers death on an external entity gave Jory some kind of comfort. No one in the town wanted to tell her the truth.
